articles.title: Teachers' Topic for January
articles.complete_text:
TEACHERS' TOPIC FOR JANUARY

THE RELIEF SOCIETY AND CHILD WELFARE

The Relief Society has always been interested in child welfare. From the very beginning of the organization, the sisters have been concerned with the care and protection of children. This interest has manifested itself in many ways—through the establishment of nurseries, the care of orphans, the promotion of better health conditions for children, and the education of mothers in child care.

In recent years this interest has taken on new phases. The Relief Society has cooperated with other agencies in promoting child welfare legislation. It has supported the establishment of juvenile courts, the passage of child labor laws, and the creation of agencies for the protection of dependent and delinquent children.

The Relief Society has also been active in promoting better health conditions for children. It has supported the establishment of child health centers, the employment of public health nurses, and the promotion of health education in the schools.

In the field of education, the Relief Society has been interested in the establishment of kindergartens, the improvement of school conditions, and the promotion of adult education.

The Relief Society has also been concerned with the moral welfare of children. It has supported the establishment of playgrounds and recreation centers, the promotion of wholesome entertainment for children, and the protection of children from harmful influences.

In all of these activities, the Relief Society has worked in cooperation with other agencies interested in child welfare. It has recognized that the welfare of children is not the responsibility of any one organization, but requires the cooperation of all agencies working for the betterment of society.

The Relief Society's interest in child welfare is based upon its fundamental belief in the importance of the home and the family. It recognizes that the welfare of children depends primarily upon the welfare of the home, and that any program for child welfare must begin with the strengthening of family life.

For this reason, the Relief Society has always emphasized the importance of education for mothers. It has provided instruction in child care, home management, and family relationships. It has sought to help mothers understand their responsibilities and to equip them for their important work.

The Relief Society's program for child welfare is thus a comprehensive one. It includes not only direct service to children, but also education for parents, cooperation with other agencies, and support for legislation designed to protect and promote the welfare of children.

In the coming year, the Relief Society will continue its interest in child welfare. It will seek to expand its services to children and families, to improve its educational program for mothers, and to strengthen its cooperation with other agencies working in this field.
articles.notes: Found the Teachers' Topic for January on page 580. This appears to be educational content for Relief Society teachers about child welfare work.
